content.text: Computer-use APIs are the programmatic interface through which an AI model requests and receives control over a desktop or browser. Rather than a model that infers and explains what should happen, a computer-use model returns structured tool_use blocks — each describing a precise input event — and expects a screenshot of the resulting screen state as the tool_result. The Anthropic computer-use API, released in late 2024 and updated through 2025, is the most widely adopted public interface for this pattern.

attrs.alt: Computer-use API architecture: Your app calls Anthropic API, receives tool_use blocks, dispatches actions, returns screenshots as tool_results
attrs.title: Computer-Use API Architecture

content.text: A computer-use API exposes a tool definition that teaches the model it can interact with a graphical environment. Instead of generating natural-language text describing what to click, the model emits a JSON block like {"action": "left_click", "coordinate": [760, 430]}, and your code physically performs that action. The model never directly controls the OS; it requests actions through a defined schema, and your executor validates and dispatches each request.

The Anthropic computer-use tool is declared in the tools array of a Messages API request with type "computer_20250124". The display_width_px and display_height_px fields tell the model the resolution of the screen it is operating on, so it can accurately reason about pixel positions. The model must be one of the claude-opus-4-5 family (or equivalent); smaller models lack the visual reasoning capability needed for accurate element grounding.

Before computer-use APIs, automating GUIs required either bespoke RPA recordings (brittle to UI changes) or accessibility-tree traversal (limited to applications that expose a11y metadata). Computer-use APIs generalise automation to any application visible on screen: legacy desktop tools, web apps with complex DOM, image editors, and development environments are all equally automatable. The API defines a stable contract between the planning model and the execution layer, enabling the planning model to be swapped independently of the executor.
Works on any visible application — no accessibility tree, no selectors, no scraping: the model sees what a human sees.
Stable API contract — action schemas are versioned (e.g. computer_20250124), allowing executor code to evolve independently.
Model-side grounding — the vision model resolves 'the Submit button' to pixel coordinates, removing that logic from your code.
Structured output — tool_use JSON is machine-parseable; no regex needed to extract actions from prose.
Composable — the computer tool can be combined with other tools (file I/O, web search) in the same API call.
Audit trail — each tool_use input block is a self-describing log entry with action type and coordinates.
Screenshot token cost — each 1920x1080 PNG encodes to roughly 800-1500 tokens; a 30-step task consumes ~40K input tokens in images alone.
Latency — one round trip (screenshot → API call → action dispatch → new screenshot) takes 2-5 seconds; long tasks take minutes.
Resolution coupling — display_width_px must match the actual capture resolution exactly or coordinates will be systematically off.
Beta API — the computer-use beta may introduce breaking changes; lock to a specific beta version string.
No streaming actions — tool_use blocks arrive together at end_of_turn, not streamed; the executor must wait for the full response.
Context window growth — a long session accumulates screenshots; without truncation the conversation hits the 200K context limit.
Safety filters — the model may refuse to perform actions it judges harmful, even benign automations that resemble sensitive tasks.

The API follows a modified tool-use protocol. You declare the computer tool in tools. The model responds with stop_reason="tool_use" and a content array containing one or more tool_use blocks. Each block has a name ("computer"), an id (used to correlate tool_results), and an input object with action and action-specific fields. Your code dispatches the actions and returns a user turn containing tool_result content blocks, each linking to a tool_use id and containing the post-action screenshot. The model then decides the next step.

The key difference from standard tool use is that the result is always an image. The model cannot receive textual feedback about whether the action succeeded; it must infer success or failure from the new screenshot. This means the executor must capture a fresh screenshot immediately after every action, even a keystroke that produces no visual change.

The diagram above shows the full request/response cycle. Your application sends a Messages API request with the computer tool and an initial screenshot. The API returns tool_use blocks. Your executor dispatches each action and collects a post-action screenshot as the tool_result. The conversation repeats until the model emits stop_reason="end_turn", signalling it considers the task complete.
Tasks that span multiple applications and cannot be automated through a single programmatic API.
Applications with no REST or GraphQL API and no accessible DOM (compiled desktop apps, old ERP systems).
Building AI assistants that operate the entire desktop on behalf of a user.
Integration testing of GUI applications from a user-perspective scenario.
Exploratory automation where the exact sequence of UI interactions is not known in advance.
Demonstrating AI capabilities — computer-use agents produce visually compelling evidence of task completion.

Install the Anthropic SDK and set your API key. Declare the computer tool in every API request. Capture the screen before the first turn and after every action dispatch. Return screenshots as base64 PNG images inside tool_result blocks.

pip install anthropic pillow pyautogui
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# The API returns a stop_reason of "tool_use" when it wants to act.
# stop_reason "end_turn" means the model is done with the task.

def step(messages: list) -> tuple[list, bool]:
    """
    Run one API call.  Returns (updated_messages, is_done).
    """
    resp = client.beta.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        tools=[{
            "type": "computer_20250124",
            "name": "computer",
            "display_width_px": 1280,
            "display_height_px": 800,
        }],
        messages=messages,
        betas=["computer-use-2025-01-24"],
    )

    messages = messages + [{"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content}]

    if resp.stop_reason == "end_turn":
        return messages, True

    # stop_reason == "tool_use" — collect the action names for logging
    for block in resp.content:
        if block.type == "tool_use":
            print(f"  action={block.input.get('action')} "
                  f"coord={block.input.get('coordinate')}")

    return messages, False


def check_error_response(resp_content: list) -> str | None:
    """
    Detect refusals and safety stops embedded in text blocks.
    Returns the warning string if found, else None.
    """
    for block in resp_content:
        if hasattr(block, "text"):
            if "I cannot" in block.text or "I'm not able" in block.text:
                return block.text
    return None

import anthropic, base64, io
from PIL import ImageGrab

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def make_screenshot_message(task_text: str) -> dict:
    """Build the user turn with screen image + text instruction."""
    img = ImageGrab.grab()
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    img.save(buf, format="PNG")
    b64 = base64.standard_b64encode(buf.getvalue()).decode()
    return {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [
            {"type": "image",
             "source": {"type": "base64", "media_type": "image/png", "data": b64}},
            {"type": "text", "text": task_text},
        ],
    }

def make_tool_result(tool_use_id: str, screenshot_b64: str) -> dict:
    """
    After executing an action, wrap the resulting screenshot as a
    tool_result message that maps to the tool_use_id.
    """
    return {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [{
            "type": "tool_result",
            "tool_use_id": tool_use_id,
            "content": [{
                "type": "image",
                "source": {
                    "type": "base64",
                    "media_type": "image/png",
                    "data": screenshot_b64,
                },
            }],
        }],
    }

# Build a complete two-turn conversation manually:
initial_msg = make_screenshot_message("Click the search bar at the top of the screen.")

resp1 = client.beta.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[{"type": "computer_20250124", "name": "computer",
            "display_width_px": 1920, "display_height_px": 1080}],
    messages=[initial_msg],
    betas=["computer-use-2025-01-24"],
)

# Execute the action (omitted for brevity), then capture new screenshot
import pyautogui, time
for block in resp1.content:
    if block.type == "tool_use" and block.input.get("action") == "left_click":
        coord = block.input["coordinate"]
        pyautogui.click(coord[0], coord[1])
        time.sleep(0.4)

img2 = ImageGrab.grab()
buf2 = io.BytesIO(); img2.save(buf2, format="PNG")
b64_2 = base64.standard_b64encode(buf2.getvalue()).decode()

tool_use_id = next(b.id for b in resp1.content if b.type == "tool_use")
result_msg = make_tool_result(tool_use_id, b64_2)

resp2 = client.beta.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[{"type": "computer_20250124", "name": "computer",
            "display_width_px": 1920, "display_height_px": 1080}],
    messages=[initial_msg,
               {"role": "assistant", "content": resp1.content},
               result_msg],
    betas=["computer-use-2025-01-24"],
)
print([b.type for b in resp2.content])

A financial services firm used the Anthropic computer-use API to automate monthly report generation from a Windows-only portfolio management system that had no REST API. The agent connected via RDP, navigated the application's nested menu structure, exported each portfolio section to Excel, and assembled the final report. Because the API's tool_use schema made every action explicit, the compliance team could audit the full action log against the expected workflow steps. The automation reduced the manual effort from four hours to eighteen minutes per report cycle.
Passing the wrong display dimensions — if display_width_px is 1280 but the screenshot is 1920 pixels wide, all coordinates will be offset by a 1.5x factor.
Reusing stale screenshots — feeding the same screenshot multiple times in a row makes the model think the screen is frozen.
Missing betas header — omitting betas=["computer-use-2025-01-24"] causes a validation error; the tool type requires the beta flag.
Not handling multiple tool_use blocks — one model response can contain several tool_use blocks that must all be dispatched and acknowledged.
Ignoring stop_reason — proceeding to dispatch actions when stop_reason is "end_turn" (no tool_use blocks) causes a key error.
Truncating messages without keeping tool_result pairs — every tool_use in the assistant turn must have a matching tool_result in the next user turn or the API returns an error.
Always pass the current screenshot in the first user turn so the model has visual context before it decides on any action.
Verify that display_width_px and display_height_px in the tool definition match the actual capture dimensions at runtime.
Implement a conversation rolling window: keep the last N screenshot pairs to avoid exceeding the 200K context window.
Use system prompt instructions to constrain the model's action space — for example, forbid typing passwords in visible fields.
Log every tool_use input block with a timestamp and a hash of the post-action screenshot for auditability.
Test with claude-opus-4-5 for reliability; benchmark with smaller models only if latency or cost requires it.
Pin the beta version string — do not use a generic "latest" alias so upgrades are explicit and tested.

Each API call with a 1080p screenshot consumes approximately 1200-1600 image tokens plus response tokens. At claude-opus-4-5 pricing this is a significant per-step cost. Strategies to reduce cost and latency: use 1280x800 resolution (roughly 60% fewer image tokens), cache static parts of the UI across turns using prompt caching, and offload routine sub-tasks (typing known text, invoking known keyboard shortcuts) to the executor directly instead of asking the model to emit them.

Parallelism is achievable by running multiple agent instances in separate Xvfb virtual displays, each handling a different top-level task. Coordinate sharing between agents requires a distributed lock so two agents do not simultaneously write to the same file or form.

Computer-use APIs amplify the capabilities of a model in ways that carry real security implications. An adversarial prompt injected into a web page or document the model reads on screen could instruct it to take destructive actions. Defense measures include:
Run the agent in a sandboxed VM or container with no access to credentials, production data, or destructive commands.
Implement a human-in-the-loop review gate for high-risk action types (file deletion, form submission, credential entry).
Validate that tool_use coordinates fall within expected screen regions before dispatching — reject clicks outside the application window.
Set a conservative max_steps limit and a wall-clock timeout; an agent stuck in a loop can rack up significant API costs.
Never log raw screenshots containing visible passwords, tokens, or PII to persistent storage without encryption.
Pin the model version in the API call to avoid behavior changes when Anthropic releases new model updates.
Computer-use APIs expose a tool definition that lets a vision model emit structured mouse/keyboard actions instead of prose.
The Anthropic API uses type computer_20250124 and requires the betas=["computer-use-2025-01-24"] flag; display dimensions must match the actual capture.
The tool_result for every computer tool_use must be a screenshot image, not text — the model infers success from the visual state.
Image tokens dominate cost and context; reduce screenshot resolution and implement a rolling message window for long tasks.
Run agents in sandboxed environments and validate action coordinates before dispatch to limit security exposure.
The API is beta — pin the version string and test upgrades explicitly rather than assuming backward compatibility.
For production use, combine computer-use with a human review gate for irreversible actions.
